title: Friedrich Christoph Schlosser

infobox.value: [University of Giessen](/wiki/University_of_Giessen "University of Giessen") [University of Göttingen](/wiki/University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen "University of Göttingen")

[Gottlieb Jakob Planck](/wiki/Gottlieb_Jakob_Planck "Gottlieb Jakob Planck") [Ludwig Timotheus Spittler](/wiki/Ludwig_Timotheus_Spittler "Ludwig Timotheus Spittler") [Johann Gottfried Eichhorn](/wiki/Johann_Gottfried_Eichhorn "Johann Gottfried Eichhorn")

(17 November 1776 – 23 September 1861) was a German [historian](/wiki/Historian "Historian"), Professor of History at the University of Heidelberg and a Privy Councillor in [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia").[[1]](#cite_note-1)
*History of the Eighteenth Century and of the Nineteenth till the overthrow of the French Empire* by F. C. Schlosser, English edition 1845, 8 volumes.

German historian and scholar (1776–1861)

**Friedrich Christoph Schlosser** (17 November 1776 – 23 September 1861) was a German [historian](/wiki/Historian "Historian"), Professor of History at the University of Heidelberg and a Privy Councillor in [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Friedrich_Christoph_Schlosser&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Schlosser.JPG/250px-Schlosser.JPG)](/wiki/File:Schlosser.JPG)

The grave in Heidelberg

He was born at [Jever](/wiki/Jever "Jever") in the [District of Friesland](/wiki/Friesland_(district) "Friesland (district)"). He studied [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology"), mainly at [Göttingen](/wiki/University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen "University of Göttingen"), and then tutored privately. Turning to the study of history, he became and remained for a quarter of a century the most popular German historian.

## Work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Friedrich_Christoph_Schlosser&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Work")]

In 1807, inspired by his study of [Dante](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri"), he published his first work *Abélard und Dulcin*, a defence of [scholasticism](/wiki/Scholasticism "Scholasticism") and [medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") thought. Two years later biographical studies of [Theodore Beza](/wiki/Theodore_Beza "Theodore Beza") and [Peter Martyr Vermili](/wiki/Peter_Martyr_Vermili "Peter Martyr Vermili") (*Leben des Theodor de Bela und des Peter Martyr Vermili*, Heidelberg, 1809) revealed more great scholarship. In 1812 his *History of the Iconoclastic Emperors of the East* (*Geschichte der bilderstürmenden Kaiser des oströmischen Reichs*) was published, in which he contradicted some points in [Edward Gibbon](/wiki/Edward_Gibbon "Edward Gibbon")'s highly opinionated work and sought to avoid painting the past in present-day colours. It won him the favour of Archbishop [Karl Theodor Dalberg](/wiki/Karl_Theodor_Dalberg "Karl Theodor Dalberg") and secured him a Professorship in the [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt") Lyceum.

In 1815 the first volume of his *World History* (*Weltgeschichte in zusammenhängender Erzählung*) was published. This work, though never completed, extended through many volumes, bespeaking an inexhaustible energy and a vast erudition. A translation of the pedagogical handbook of [Vincent of Beauvais](/wiki/Vincent_of_Beauvais "Vincent of Beauvais") and the accompanying monograph are still considered of value.

The next noteworthy work was a history of antiquity and its culture (*Universalhistorische Übersicht der Geschichte der alten Welt und ihrer Kultur*, 1st part, 1826; 2nd part, 1834), which, while revealing little knowledge of the new criticism of sources inaugurated by [F.A. Wolf](/wiki/Friedrich_August_Wolf "Friedrich August Wolf") and [B.G. Niebuhr](/wiki/Barthold_Georg_Niebuhr "Barthold Georg Niebuhr"), won its way by its unique handling of the subject and its grand style. In 1823 he published in two volumes a *Geschichte des 18ten Jahrhunderts*. This work, enlarged and improved, appeared in six volumes as *Geschichte des 18ten Jahrhunderts und des 19ten bis zum Sturz des französischen Kaiserreichs* (1836–1848). The history had a most extraordinary success, especially among the common people, since the author boldly and sternly sat in judgment upon men and events, and in his judgments voiced the feelings of the German nation in his day. It has been translated into English by D. Davison (8 vols, 1843–1852). Finally, Schlosser undertook a popular *World History for the German People* (*Weltgeschichte für das deutsche Volk*, 1844–1857), which also enjoyed the favour of those for whom it was written.

He left Frankfurt in 1819 to become professor of history at [Heidelberg](/wiki/University_of_Heidelberg "University of Heidelberg") where he resided until his death. He was an elected member of the Royal Danish Academy of Sciences of Copenhagen, and of the Society of Literature of [Leyden](/wiki/Leyden "Leyden") and other learned societies.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

### Works in English translation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Friedrich_Christoph_Schlosser&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Works in English translation")]

* *History of the Eighteenth Century and of the Nineteenth Century Till the Overthrow of the French Empire,* volumes 1–8, Chapman and Hall. London, 1843–1852.

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Friedrich_Christoph_Schlosser&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Criticism")]

Schlosser stands apart from the movement towards scientific history in Germany in the 19th century.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Refusing to limit himself to political history, as did [Leopold von Ranke](/wiki/Leopold_von_Ranke "Leopold von Ranke"), he never learned to handle his literary sources with the care of the scientific historian. History was to him, as it had been to [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero"), a school for morals; but he had perhaps a juster conception than Ranke of the breadth and scope of the historian's field.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Friedrich_Christoph_Schlosser&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Notes")]
